intake.finding: ChromeStatus entry created 2026-04-08 by yyanagisawa@google.com with owner, Blink component (Blink>Workers), category (Security), spec link, motivation, risks, measurement counters and a tracking bug (crbug 40051700). Problem statement is concrete: data: URL Dedicated/Shared Workers inherit the creator origin, deviating from the HTML spec and enabling BroadcastChannel/storage access. Entry diagnosis and owner assignment are present.
intake.limitations: Cached record is accurate as of 2026-06-15; no explainer_links and no initial_public_proposal_url were recorded, so the earliest problem framing outside ChromeStatus is not captured.

incubation.finding: The problem (same-origin data leakage via BroadcastChannel/LocalStorage/IndexedDB) and the spec anchor (HTML worker settings, Step 3) are clear, and the underlying standards question is long-standing (WHATWG html#1319, 2016). However there is no explainer, no use-case brief, no developer research, and the intent explicitly states it is 'currently unclear how many developers intentionally rely on the existing non-standard origin inheritance behavior.' The page-load affected surface is now exactly measured (counter 5568 DataUrlDedicatedWorker at 0.00116307 (0.116%) and 5569 DataUrlSharedWorker at 0.00016675 (0.0167%) of measured page loads at M150 stable), confirming the owner's order-of-magnitude estimates; both counters have declined from 2025 peaks (dedicated 0.340% in 2025-06; shared 0.0531% in 2025-05), so the affected surface was shrinking as ship approached. But these counters fire on creation of a data: URL worker regardless of origin behavior, so they quantify the affected surface, not intentional reliance on creator-origin inheritance; that denominator was left unresolved at incubation and remained so through ship.
incubation.limitations: No explainer or public incubation venue; the page-load affected surface is now exactly measured but intentional reliance on the non-standard behavior was never measured, only bounded above by page-load use-counter prevalence.

prototype.finding: The change is implemented in shared core Chromium worker infrastructure (content layer) behind the kDataUrlWorkerOpaqueOrigin feature flag, with a WPT test (opaque-origin.html) covering data: URL dedicated and shared workers. There is no separate prototype artifact, no task-based friction log, and no framework/server integration spike.
prototype.limitations: No prototype document or integration matrix exists; verification is limited to the single WPT file plus the author's later site testing.

developerTrials.finding: No developer trial (behind-a-flag external cohort) was run, and none was warranted: this is a default-on spec-alignment security behavior change, not an opt-in capability for developers to evaluate against tasks. The cached stages (types 110-160) contain no dev-trial URLs, origin trial IDs or instructions.
developerTrials.limitations: Not-relevant because a developer trial is not a meaningful phase for a default-on behavior fix; this is not used to mean untested (the author instead did direct site inspection).

wideReview.finding: Wide review effectively occurred on the blink-dev Intent to Ship thread (4Hy56DZjF34): Gecko and WebKit are recorded as already shipping the spec behavior (Chrome was the outlier), reviewers (Mike Taylor, Rick Byers, Vladimir Levin, Chris Harrelson) pressed on debuggability and non-enterprise safety, and 4 LGTMs were granted by 2026-05-07. But TAG review status is 'Pending' and was not completed at ship, web-develop signals are 'No signals,' and no Mozilla/WebKit position document was linked (only ChromeStatus ff_views/safari_views fields).
wideReview.limitations: No formal TAG review; no attributable web/framework developer signal; interop claim rests on ChromeStatus position fields plus a WPT test rather than a cited standards-venue confirmation.

experiment.finding: No Origin Trial was conducted or appropriate. The change ships default-on for all users, guarded by the kDataUrlWorkerOpaqueOrigin flag and an enterprise policy escape hatch (DataUrlInWebWorkerOpaqueOriginEnabled); origintrial is false and no OT stage data exists. There were no stated field-experiment hypotheses because the question is conformance/security, not adoption signal collection.
experiment.limitations: Not-relevant because no field experiment was needed; not used to mean untested.

prepareToShip.finding: The Intent to Ship provides motivation, interop/compat/security analysis, activation risks, debuggability, measurement counters (5568/5569), an all-six-Blink-platform statement, a rollout plan ('ship enabled for all users'), a feature flag and a temporary enterprise escape hatch. Unusually for a change this small, the author then tested ~182 sites using data: URL workers (63 with no worker started, 119 with no detected BroadcastChannel/localStorage/IndexedDB/self.origin issues; dynamic trap injection on more than half), which satisfied reviewers. Estimated milestones (desktop/Android/WebView = 150) match the actual ship. The page-load affected-surface figures underpinning the 'moderate' compatibility rating (~0.13% dedicated / ~0.01% shared as cited by the owner) are now independently confirmed by the cached timelines (0.116% dedicated / 0.0167% shared at M150 stable).
prepareToShip.limitations: Formal tag/security/privacy review statuses are all 'Pending' in the cached record at ship time; the site test was the author's own (not independently reproduced). The confirmed page-load figures measure worker creation (the affected surface), not intentional reliance.

release.finding: The feature shipped in Chrome 150 stable (desktop/Android/WebView), with stable promoted 2026-06-30. The Chrome for Developers release notes describe it accurately under 'Security and Web Speech', naming the behavior change, the spec, tracking bug #40051700, and the storage-partition preservation. An enterprise policy (DataUrlInWebWorkerOpaqueOriginEnabled) is documented on Chrome Enterprise / ADMX mirrors and inherited by Microsoft Edge.
release.limitations: No dedicated developer article, migration sample or FAQ beyond the release-notes paragraph; the ADMX policy mirror text says 'milestone 149' whereas the authoritative Chrome Enterprise page and the release say 150 (a wording-drift gap).

adoption.finding: 'Adoption' is not a meaningful lifecycle phase here: this is a default-on security/conformance behavior change, not a developer-facing capability with an adoption funnel, so no adoption phase or developer trial was run. First-party Chrome usage telemetry does exist for the affected surface — counters 5568 (DataUrlDedicatedWorker) and 5569 (DataUrlSharedWorker), mapping multiple-counters with each counter scope exact — but they fire whenever a data: URL dedicated/shared worker is created, regardless of its origin, so they measure the page-load population the change affects, not adoption of the opaque-origin default. At Chrome 150 stable (2026-06-30) that affected surface was 0.00116307 (0.116%) of measured page loads for dedicated workers and 0.00016675 (0.0167%) for shared workers; day30/day90/day180 launch-relative checkpoints are null (only ~3 weeks post-stable). The relevant post-ship question remains compatibility/breakage, assessed under support/implementationQuality.
adoption.limitations: Not-relevant by design (not 'untested'): Chrome usage telemetry exists for the affected surface but it is not adoption telemetry for a default-on behavior change. No unique-site, production-deployment, retention, task-success, user-benefit, or predeclared adoption-target evidence exists.

support.finding: About three weeks have elapsed since Chrome 150 stable (2026-06-30); no support-issue patterns, regressions or breakage reports attributable to this change were found in the search performed at the prior cutoff (2026-07-20). The cached usage counters (5568/5569) are the only newer evidence: post-stable worker-creation prevalence is roughly flat to slightly down (dedicated 0.00116307 on 2026-06-30 to 0.00115679 on 2026-07-21; shared 0.00016675 to 0.0001523), but because the counters fire on worker creation regardless of origin behavior, they are orthogonal to breakage from the origin change and neither confirm nor refute it. The known debuggability risk (BroadcastChannel fails silently; storage throws SecurityError; self.origin returns null) is acknowledged but unmitigated in product for the silent case.
support.limitations: Too early to assess support burden; the breakage search was not refreshed after 2026-07-20, and absence of public breakage reports plus flat worker-creation counters are not confirmation of no breakage (the counters are insensitive to origin-behavior failures). The crbug/issue tracker could not be retrieved directly to check for post-ship reports.

deprecation.finding: The legacy same-origin-inheritance behavior is effectively being removed, with a reverse-escape-hatch: the enterprise policy DataUrlInWebWorkerOpaqueOriginEnabled, when Disabled, restores legacy inheritance, and it is explicitly temporary (to be removed in milestone 157). Detection guidance exists by API (SecurityError for storage; null for self.origin; silent for BroadcastChannel). Enterprise notification began at milestone 148.
deprecation.limitations: The silent BroadcastChannel failure path has no console/devtools signal, weakening detection; first_enterprise_notification_milestone is 148 but the policy mirror mis-states 149; removal-at-157 is a future job not yet tracked as a deprecation milestone.

developerValue.finding: Mixed. Developers gain cross-browser consistency (Chrome now matches Firefox, Safari and the HTML spec) and removal of a same-origin data-leakage footgun, but there is no new capability; the population creating data: URL workers is now exactly measured as small (0.116% dedicated / 0.0167% shared of measured page loads at M150 stable), yet the subset intentionally relying on same-origin inheritance — which faces a forced migration to postMessage() or regular script URLs, with BroadcastChannel breakage being silent — was never measured.
developerValue.limitations: Net value depends on how many relied on the old behavior intentionally, which remains unmeasured; the measured 0.116%/0.0167% is worker-creation prevalence (affected surface), not reliance.

endUserImpact.finding: Mixed. The change is a well-reasoned security improvement that isolates data: URL workers from creator same-origin state (BroadcastChannel/storage), with low observed breakage so far; but no concrete attack prevented is documented and there is residual breakage risk for end users on affected sites.
endUserImpact.limitations: Security benefit is reasoned rather than measured; post-ship user-facing impact data is not yet available; the usage counters measure worker creation and are insensitive to origin-behavior breakage, so they do not inform end-user impact directly.

adoption.finding: Unscored. This default-on security/conformance behavior change has no adoption funnel and no predeclared adoption target, and the mapped usage counters (5568/5569, scope exact, mapping multiple-counters) are not adoption telemetry: they fire on creation of a data: URL worker regardless of origin, measuring the page-load population the change affects. That affected surface is small and now exactly measured — 0.00116307 (0.116%) dedicated-worker and 0.00016675 (0.0167%) shared-worker page loads at M150 stable (2026-06-30), roughly flat to slightly down through 2026-07-21 (0.00115679 / 0.0001523); day30/day90/day180 checkpoints are not yet available. With no target, no production-deployment or retention evidence, and counters that quantify affected surface rather than adoption of the default, adoption is not scored. Low affected-surface prevalence is not treated as failure (it is a compatibility-denominator signal, not an adoption result), and high prevalence would not be success either.
adoption.limitations: Not scoreable: no predeclared adoption target and no unique-site/production/retention/task-success/user-benefit evidence; the counters measure worker-creation prevalence (affected surface), not adoption of the opaque-origin behavior. 'Unscored' (not 'not-relevant' and not 'mixed') is used because usage telemetry now exists even though it is not adoption telemetry and no threshold was declared.

interoperability.finding: Success. This is the clearest win: the change aligns Chrome with the HTML Living Standard and with Firefox and Safari, which already assign opaque origins to data: URL workers; Chrome was the documented outlier. The WPT opaque-origin.html test (authored 2022) explicitly asserts data: URL dedicated/shared workers 'should have opaque origins per the HTML spec,' so it would have failed in pre-fix Chrome and now passes; the 2022 BroadcastChannel-in-opaque-origin work established the self-contained opaque-origin semantics this depends on. Microsoft Edge inherits the behavior via Chromium.
interoperability.limitations: Interop positions rest on ChromeStatus fields plus the WPT test rather than cited Mozilla/WebKit position docs; Safari had a known prior bug (webkit.org/b/238090) on opaque-origin BroadcastChannel isolation that is a related but distinct aspect.

implementationQuality.finding: Mixed. Strong mitigation: implemented in shared core worker infra (uniform across all six Blink platforms), guarded by feature flag + temporary enterprise policy, backed by a WPT test, and supported by pre-ship site testing on ~182 sites; the page-load affected surface underpinning the 'moderate' compatibility rating is now exactly measured (0.116% dedicated / 0.0167% shared at M150 stable), confirming the owner's estimates. Weaknesses: formal tag/security/privacy review statuses are all 'Pending' at ship, intentional reliance on the non-standard behavior (the true breakage denominator) remains unmeasured, and there is no post-ship conformance/regression evidence yet — and the usage counters are insensitive to origin-behavior breakage, so they cannot substitute for it.
implementationQuality.limitations: Reviews pending in the record; conformance verified via one WPT file; no independent regression run observed; the page-load affected surface is now exactly measured but reliance and breakage are not.

evidenceAndCommunication.finding: Mixed. The intent-thread Q&A was unusually thorough for a change of this size (explicit failure-mode analysis, site-testing results, 4 LGTMs) and the release-notes entry is accurate; moreover the owner's pre-ship use-counter citations (~0.13% dedicated / ~0.01% shared page loads) are now independently confirmed by the cached timelines (0.116% dedicated / 0.0167% shared at M150 stable), validating the telemetry basis for the compatibility rating. But there is no explainer, no TAG review, no developer migration doc/sample, web-develop signals are explicitly 'No signals,' the true compatibility denominator (intentional reliance on creator-origin inheritance) is an unmeasured upper bound, and the owner-cited BroadcastChannel counter 5856 is not present in the feature's cached measurement set. An ADMX policy mirror states milestone 149 while authoritative sources say 150.
evidenceAndCommunication.limitations: Page-load affected surface is now exactly measured, but the reliance estimate remains the author's own upper bound; the BroadcastChannel-specific counter 5856 is owner-cited and unverified; a documentation/source-consistency drift exists in policy mirrors.

support.finding: Unscored. Only ~3 weeks post-stable at the cutoff; no attributable support issues, regressions or breakage reports found in the search performed at the prior cutoff (2026-07-20). The cached usage counters (5568/5569) are the only newer evidence and show roughly flat worker-creation prevalence post-stable, but they fire on worker creation regardless of origin behavior and are therefore insensitive to breakage from the origin change, so they neither confirm nor refute compatibility. The acknowledged silent-failure mode for BroadcastChannel is an open support risk.
support.limitations: Insufficient elapsed time and no retrievable tracker data to score support burden or issue resolution; the breakage search was not refreshed after 2026-07-20 and the usage counters cannot stand in for breakage evidence.

overall.finding: Mixed overall. A well-justified, low-risk spec-alignment security fix that closes an interop outlier (success on interoperability), with strong pre-ship site testing and layered mitigation; the page-load population the change affects is now exactly measured as small (0.116% dedicated / 0.0167% shared at M150 stable). But it ships with pending formal reviews, no developer migration artifact, an unmeasured intentional-reliance denominator, an unmitigated silent-failure path, and no post-ship breakage/outcome evidence yet (the usage counters are insensitive to origin-behavior breakage). Shipment is appropriate; success is not inferred from shipment.
overall.limitations: Cannot be scored success until post-ship breakage/support evidence and the M157 policy-removal deprecation are observed; the small measured affected surface bounds compatibility risk but does not measure reliance or confirm absence of breakage.

supports: Feature metadata, owner, spec link, finch name kDataUrlWorkerOpaqueOrigin, stages (no OT/dev-trial), ship targets desktop/android/webview=150, enterprise notification M148, ff/safari=Shipped/Shipping, webdev=No signals, tag/security/privacy review Pending, motivation/risks/measurement text
sources.limitations: Cached snapshot, not live; some fields (intent_thread_url per stage) are empty; is_released=false in snapshot despite ship.

sources.publisher: blink-dev (Intent to Ship: Opaque origin for data: URL Dedicated and Shared Workers)
supports: Intent to Ship 2026-04-30 by Yoshisato Yanagisawa; motivation; interop low/compat moderate risk; ~0.13% page loads data: URL dedicated workers, 0.01% shared workers; feature flag + enterprise escape hatch; ~182-site test (63 no worker, 119 no issues, dynamic trap on >half); failure modes (Storage SecurityError, BroadcastChannel silent, self.origin null); 4 LGTMs (Harrelson, Levin, Taylor, Byers) by 2026-05-07; WPT test confirmation
sources.limitations: Reliance/site-test figures are the author's self-reported analysis; BroadcastChannel-specific prevalence cited via metric 5856 not independently verified (and 5856 is not in the feature's cached measurement set).

supports: Chrome 150 stable date 2026-06-30; 'Opaque origin for data: URLs' described under Security; spec/tracking bug #40051700; storage-partition preservation statement
sources.limitations: Single paragraph; no migration sample or FAQ; no breakage metrics.

supports: Living Standard authority for worker script settings (Step 3 = opaque origin handling) cited as the basis for the change; 'Published standard' maturity
sources.limitations: Spec section is large; the precise Step 3 opaque-origin algorithm was not extracted verbatim due to output truncation.

supports: 4 promise_tests in opaque-origin.html including 'BroadcastChannel messages from data URL dedicated/shared workers should be self-contained'; comments state data: URL workers 'should have opaque origins per the HTML spec,' confirming the test encodes the spec behavior Chrome was violating
sources.limitations: Current master read; exact authoring commit/date not captured; demonstrates expected behavior, not live pass/fail per browser (dashboard did not render).

supports: Long-standing spec question about BroadcastChannel in opaque/unique origins (2016); notes Firefox throws from sandboxed iframe; establishes the standards ambiguity this change leans into
sources.limitations: Issue body only; closed; does not by itself state the data: URL worker origin rule.

sources.publisher: blink-dev (PSA: Enabling BroadcastChannel usage in opaque origin contexts)
supports: M102 change allowing BroadcastChannel in opaque-origin contexts; establishes that opaque-origin BroadcastChannel is self-contained (cannot reach other browsing contexts) — the mechanism behind the silent-failure risk; references WPT opaque-origin.html and webkit.org/b/238090
sources.limitations: Distinct from (but prerequisite to understanding) the data: URL worker change; pre-dates this feature by ~4 years.

supports: Policy DataUrlInWebWorkerOpaqueOriginEnabled: Enabled/unset = secure opaque-origin default; Disabled = legacy inheritance; intended temporary, removed in milestone 157; first notified M148
sources.limitations: Third-party mirror; mirror text says default change is 'milestone 149' which conflicts with authoritative Chrome 150 sources (likely a mirror wording error).

supports: Authoritative policy exists; search snippet references 'Chrome 150 (Beta)', correcting the mirror's 149; confirms enterprise escape hatch is part of the launch
sources.limitations: Full page timed out on retrieval; details taken from search snippet corroborated by S8.

supports: Chrome 150 promoted to stable for Windows/Mac/Linux (150.0.7871.46/.47) in June 2026; confirms ship timing
sources.limitations: Generic stable post; does not enumerate this specific feature.

supports: Edge (Chromium) documents DataUrlInWebWorkerOpaqueOriginEnabled with the same semantics — corroborates cross-engine behavior inheritance
sources.limitations: Retrieved via search snippet only; page body not fetched.

sources.publisher: Chrome Platform Status (use counters, popularity chart page)
supports: The page-load affected-surface figures the owner relied on for the 'moderate' compatibility rating, and the owner-cited BroadcastChannel-specific counter 5856
sources.limitations: The JS-rendered popularity chart page was not directly scraped at intake; the underlying 5568/5569 timeline data is now independently cached and observed (S13/S14). Counter 5856 (BroadcastChannel-in-data: URL-worker prevalence) is cited only by the feature owner in the intent thread and is NOT present in the feature's cached ChromeStatus measurement set, so it remains unverified. Page-load prevalence is an upper bound on reliance, not measured reliance.

sources.publisher: Chrome Platform Status (DataUrlDedicatedWorker use-counter timeline, cached)
supports: Counter 5568 DataUrlDedicatedWorker, scope exact (mapping multiple-counters with 5569). At Chrome 150 stable (2026-06-30) the counter fired on 0.00116307 (0.116%) of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads; latest point 0.00115679 (0.116%) on 2026-07-21; first positive 0.000013 on 2025-05-15; peak 0.003403 (0.340%) on 2025-06-05; day30/day90/day180 launch-relative checkpoints are null (only ~3 weeks post-stable).
sources.limitations: Values are the share of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads on which the counter fired at least once — not unique developers, sites, users, successful tasks, satisfaction, production deployment, or end-user benefit. The counter fires on creation of a data: URL dedicated worker regardless of its origin, so it quantifies the page-load population the change affects (the compatibility denominator), not adoption of the opaque-origin default and not breakage from it. Cached timeline snapshot (evidence/metrics/timelines/feature-5568.json, sha256 e01959597d050d6a6d3a41e7a70baad3f45bcd422f9c6593af46134ddb84c12d), not live; UMA reflects participating Chrome clients and the dominant milestone; enterprise/opt-out populations may be underrepresented.

sources.publisher: Chrome Platform Status (DataUrlSharedWorker use-counter timeline, cached)
supports: Counter 5569 DataUrlSharedWorker, scope exact (mapping multiple-counters with 5568). At Chrome 150 stable (2026-06-30) the counter fired on 0.00016675 (0.0167%) of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads; latest point 0.0001523 (0.0152%) on 2026-07-21; first positive 0.000001 on 2025-05-18; peak 0.000531 (0.0531%) on 2025-05-27; day30/day90/day180 launch-relative checkpoints are null (only ~3 weeks post-stable).
sources.limitations: Values are the share of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads on which the counter fired at least once — not unique developers, sites, users, successful tasks, satisfaction, production deployment, or end-user benefit. The counter fires on creation of a data: URL shared worker regardless of its origin, so it quantifies the page-load population the change affects (the compatibility denominator), not adoption of the opaque-origin default and not breakage from it. Cached timeline snapshot (evidence/metrics/timelines/feature-5569.json, sha256 70375db30f05c63cf35958e5b9ee147cf7de9a87788e2880af823d51e3df944f), not live; UMA reflects participating Chrome clients and the dominant milestone; enterprise/opt-out populations may be underrepresented.

successes: Unusually rigorous pre-ship validation for a small change: the owner inspected ~182 sites using data: URL workers and dynamically injected traps into more than half, which directly answered reviewer safety questions.
Correctly identified Chrome as the interop outlier and aligned to the HTML spec and to Firefox/Safari, which already ship opaque origins for data: URL workers.

Layered mitigation before stable: feature flag (kDataUrlWorkerOpaqueOrigin), a temporary enterprise escape-hatch policy (DataUrlInWebWorkerOpaqueOriginEnabled), documented per-API failure modes, and uniform implementation across all six Blink platforms.
Clear and accurate Chrome 150 release-notes entry naming the behavior change, spec and tracking bug.
The WPT test (opaque-origin.html) already encoded the spec-correct expectation, giving a concrete conformance anchor.

The feature owner's pre-ship use-counter citations (~0.13% dedicated / ~0.01% shared page loads) are independently confirmed by the cached ChromeStatus timelines (0.116% dedicated / 0.0167% shared at M150 stable), validating the telemetry basis for the 'moderate' compatibility rating.
failures: TAG review was not completed at ship (status Pending); for a change justified primarily as spec alignment, standards-venue confirmation was absent.
No explainer and no developer migration artifact (e.g., a postMessage() migration sample) shipped alongside the change.

Web-develop signals were explicitly 'No signals,' and the number of developers intentionally relying on same-origin data: URL workers was never measured — compatibility risk was rated on page-load prevalence (now exactly measured at 0.116% dedicated / 0.0167% shared at M150 stable, but still only an upper bound on reliance), not on measured intentional reliance.
The BroadcastChannel failure path is silent (no console/devtools signal), an acknowledged debuggability weakness left unmitigated in product.

A third-party enterprise-policy mirror states the default change as 'milestone 149' while authoritative Chrome sources say 150, a source-consistency drift that could mislead admins relying on the escape hatch.
No post-ship breakage or support evidence is available yet (only ~3 weeks since stable); the cached usage counters are insensitive to origin-behavior breakage and so cannot substitute for it.
counterfactuals: Measuring *intentional* reliance on same-origin data: URL workers (not just use-counter page-load %, which is now exactly measured at 0.116% dedicated / 0.0167% shared at M150 stable) before ship would have made the 'moderate' compatibility rating defensible instead of an explicitly 'unclear' denominator; the owner's site testing partially compensated but did not replace it.

Publishing a short developer migration note (move BroadcastChannel traffic to postMessage() or a regular script URL) before stable would have reduced the impact of the one silent-failure path, which is otherwise hard for developers to diagnose.

Completing a TAG review before ship would have converted the interop claim from 'Firefox/Safari already ship + a WPT test' into a standards-venue confirmation, strengthening the weakest review dimension.
Reconciling the enterprise-policy mirror text ('milestone 149') with the authoritative Chrome Enterprise page (150) before launch would have avoided confusing admins who depend on the escape hatch.

Tracking the M157 removal of DataUrlInWebWorkerOpaqueOriginEnabled as an explicit deprecation milestone at ship time would prevent the escape hatch from quietly expiring without a re-check of remaining breakage.
skillImprovements: Add a retrospective rule for default-on behavior changes that close a security hole: explicitly distinguish (a) page-load prevalence of the affected API (here now exactly measured at 0.116% dedicated / 0.0167% shared at M150 stable) from (b) measured reliance on the *non-standard* behavior being changed (still unmeasured), and treat (a) only as an upper-bound denominator — and never let an affected-surface counter stand in for breakage evidence, since for an origin-behavior change the worker is created either way.

Add a rule that for default-on behavior changes (as opposed to new opt-in capabilities), a use counter that fires on API usage regardless of the changed behavior measures the affected/compatibility surface, not adoption and not breakage; the retrospective must state this explicitly rather than implying the counter gauges the change's success.

Add a 'silent-failure debuggability' gate: when a change makes an API silently no-op (here BroadcastChannel) versus throw (Storage) versus change a return value (self.origin), require a pre-ship console/DevTools signal or a migration doc, because silent failure is the hardest path for developers to diagnose.

Require cross-checking any third-party enterprise-policy mirror against the authoritative chromeenterprise.google.com policy page before counting the escape hatch as launch-ready, to catch milestone/wording drift.
For spec-alignment interop claims, require a direct link to the standards-venue position or to a passing/failing WPT test rather than relying solely on ChromeStatus ff_views/safari_views fields.

Treat a temporary enterprise escape hatch that has a stated removal milestone (here M157) as a tracked follow-up deprecation/removal job in the retrospective, not as resolved at ship — add a regression eval that re-checks breakage before the policy is removed.

Add guidance that for small security/conformance fixes, the bar for 'no explainer / no dev trial' is acceptable, but the bar for 'no TAG review + no web-dev signal + unmeasured reliance' should trigger an explicit accepted-risk record rather than a clean LGTM.

missingEvidence: Post-ship breakage/support/regression evidence: only ~3 weeks elapsed since Chrome 150 stable (2026-06-30) and no attributable reports found in the search performed at the prior cutoff (2026-07-20); the cached usage counters (5568/5569) are insensitive to origin-behavior breakage and cannot substitute for breakage evidence, so support burden remains unscorable and the compatibility assumption is unconfirmed.

Measured denominator of developers intentionally relying on same-origin data: URL workers (was explicitly 'unclear' at ship and never resolved); the page-load affected surface is now exactly measured (0.116% dedicated / 0.0167% shared at M150 stable) but that is an upper bound on reliance, not measured reliance.

Counter 5856 (BroadcastChannel-in-data: URL-worker prevalence), cited by the feature owner for the BroadcastChannel-specific risk, is NOT present in the feature's cached ChromeStatus measurement set and remains unverified; only counters 5568/5569 are independently cached and observed (S13/S14).
TAG review (Pending at ship) and attributable Mozilla/WebKit position documents (only ChromeStatus fields + WPT test available).
Direct crbug 40051700 / issue-tracker content could not be retrieved (reader errors), so internal implementation/test/regression detail and any post-ship comments are unverified.
The M157 removal of the enterprise escape hatch is a future outcome not yet observable at the cutoff.
